title: Rectal Prolapse in Cats: Symptoms, Causes, and Emergency Care
meta_desc: If your cat has pink tissue protruding from their anus, it is likely a rectal prolapse. This is a high-priority veterinary emergency.
content_html:
What is a Rectal Prolapse?

A rectal prolapse occurs when the internal lining of the rectum or the lower part of the large intestine protrudes through the anal opening. This typically appears as a moist, tube-like, pink or red mass. It is often the result of severe straining, which can be caused by various underlying issues such as diarrhea, constipation, intestinal parasites, or urinary tract obstructions.

Is it an Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. Yes, this is a veterinary emergency. When the delicate internal tissue is exposed to the air, it can quickly become dry, irritated, and damaged. More importantly, the blood supply to the tissue can be cut off, leading to tissue death (necrosis). You should seek immediate veterinary care at an emergency clinic to prevent permanent damage and manage your cat's pain.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

If you are able to safely do so, taking a clear photo of the protrusion can be extremely helpful for the veterinary triage team. Sending a photo ahead or showing it upon arrival allows the staff to quickly assess the color, size, and condition of the tissue. This helps them determine the level of urgency and prepare the necessary treatment area before your cat is even moved into the exam room.

faqs.question: Can I try to push the tissue back in myself?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to push the tissue back in. You may cause further trauma, pain, or infection. Keep the area moist with a water-based lubricant if instructed by your vet, and head to the clinic immediately.

faqs.question: What will the veterinarian do for a prolapse?
faqs.answer: The vet will assess the health of the tissue, clean it, and usually attempt to gently replace it while the cat is sedated. In some cases, a 'purse-string' suture is used to keep the area closed while the underlying cause of straining is treated.

faqs.question: Is rectal prolapse painful for cats?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is a very painful and distressing condition. Your cat may cry, over-groom the area, or act lethargic. Professional pain management is a vital part of their treatment.
